bio.education: University of Cincinnati '01, B.S. in Criminal Justice and a minor in Law
bio.biography:
Tracey Anderson was joking around with her rowing teammates at the University of Cincinnati about becoming an Olympic bobsledder after watching the 2002 Winter Olympic Games.  When Anderson moved to Utah for her husband's job, she went to the Utah Olympic Park to inquire about the bobsled program.  She was encouraged to give the sport of skeleton a try, so Anderson attended a school and was immediately addicted.
 
About me:
Anderson graduated from the University of Cincinnati in 2001 with a B.S. in Criminal Justice and a minor in Law.  She has an Associates degree in Exercise Health and Fitness from the Utah Career College and is Stott Pilates trained, TRX certified and holds a certificate in Body Masters Nutrition.  In her free time, Anderson enjoys going to amusement parks, spending time with her son, Bauer, swimming, biking, playing hockey and hanging out with friends and family.
 
Career Highlights
  • 2010-2011 Intercontinental Cup
    -19th in Winterberg (11/10), 14th in Altenberg (12/10) 
  • 6th overall in 2009-2010 America's Cup standings
    -5th in Park City #1, 5th in Park City #2, BRONZE in Park City #3, 7th in Calgary #1, 5th in Calgary #2, 10th in Lake Placid #1, 10th in Lake Placid #2

Quick Facts
  • Mother is Nancy Anderson. Father, Michael Anderson, died in January 2010
  • Has three older sisters, Jennifer, Joelle and Amy and one older brother Joshua
  • Married Jacquelyn (Jackie) Gillum in 2020
  • Their son Michael James (Jamie) was born in 2020
  • Has a tattoo on his wrist in honor of his nephew Tristin, who is autistic
  • Hosts an annual volleyball tournament to benefit autism awareness
  • Interested in professional sports, cars and fashion
  • Left college following his junior year to play professionally with the Hyundai Capital Skywalkers in Korea
  • Has played professionally in Russia, Italy and Korea
  • At 25, was the youngest player on the 2012 U.S. Men’s Olympic Volleyball Team.
Olympic Experience

  • Years of participation: Team - Men 2010, 2014, 2018, 2022
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)

Quick Facts
    Diagnosed at birth with cerebral palsy, Michael was taught to have a huge drive and a sense of adventure
  • Growing up, his parents were determined to help give him everything he needs to adapt certain hurdles due to his cerebral palsy along with finding the joy in it
  • No matter what he’s doing, Michael always has a smile on his face and is most likely having fun
  • Having earned a Bachelors degree in Visual Communication and Design from Eastern Washington University, he loves to create and be creative in whatever he does
  • Michael is from the Pacific Northwest and has been immersed in outdoor action sports ever since he was a kid
  • Snowboarding and cycling have always been his passions
  • In 2019, Michael got introduced to the Para Track and Field world by Parasport Spokane, which provides training and competitive opportunities for athletes with disabilities
  • Though it is more popular overseas, Frame Running came into his life via Parasport Spokane
  • His frame runner helps stabilize him so he can sprint down the track without worry for losing his balance
  • Taking all his athleticism and the desire to progress from snowboarding and cycling, and pouring it into frame running led him to being the American record holder for the 100m sprint
  • In 2023, Michael was the first American male frame runner to compete at the world championship level
  • His goals are to help grow the sport and hopefully see it included in a Paralympic Games
  •  
World Championships Experience

Quick Facts

  • Sport: Wheelchair rugby

  • Specialty: Mixed wheelchair rugby

Biographical Information 

Chuck Aoki is an American wheelchair rugby player, a Paralympic medalist and one of the most recognized and decorated athletes in the sport. Aoki lives with hereditary sensory autonomic neuropathy (HSAN Type II), a rare condition affecting his ability to feel pain and temperature. This condition shaped his early experiences with sports and eventually led him to adaptive athletics.

Aoki initially began with wheelchair basketball at age seven, winning back-to-back national championships with the Jr. Rolling Timberwolves as a teenager. At 15, inspired by the documentary "Murderball," Aoki transitioned to wheelchair rugby, drawn to its intensity and camaraderie. He graduated from Southwest High School and afterward earned his master’s in public policy at the University of Minnesota. An advocate for athletes with disabilities, Aoki takes to social media to educate others on living with a disability.

Paralympic Highlights

At the London 2012 Paralympic Games, Aoki made an impressive Paralympic debut, helping guide Team USA's wheelchair rugby team to a bronze medal finish. Four years later at the Rio 2016 Paralympic Games, he elevated his team's performance, leading the United States to a silver medal in an intensely fought final match.

The Tokyo 2020 Paralympic Games marked a special milestone for Aoki, who served as Team USA's co-captain and flag bearer. He continued his stellar performance on the court, again steering the team to a silver medal.

Aoki continued his Paralympic journey, at the Paris 2024 Paralympic Games, leading Team USA to their third consecutive silver medal, and earning his fourth Paralympic medal in his career. With four Paralympic medals, Aoki became the most decorated U.S. athlete in the sport.

World Championship Highlights

Chuck Aoki has an impressive world championship career, having first joined the U.S. World Championship roster in 2010. He holds the distinction of being one of only two USA wheelchair rugby athletes to medal at four different world championships.

Early Career 

At just 17, Aoki made history as the second-youngest player ever selected for the U.S. national wheelchair rugby team in 2008-09. His international debut came at the 2009 American Zonal Championships, where he helped the U.S. secure gold. He followed this with another gold at the 2010 World Wheelchair Rugby Championship and the 2011 American Zonal Championships.

In domestic competition, Aoki's achievements include two Division I National Championships alongside his Division II title. His exceptional play earned him multiple MVP awards and the 2011 United States Quad Rugby Association (USQRA) Player of the Year honor.

He also plays professionally in Italy

bio.fun_fact: Co-founder of Culxtured, a social media company focused on covering para athletes
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Standing cross-country skiing/biathlon, 400m 
  • Nicknamed “Biathlon Bandit”
  • Parents, Rob and Kate 
  • Hobbies include horseback riding, hiking, fashion and enjoying life in the Mountain West
  • Co-founder of Culxtured (pronounced “cultured”), a social media company focused on covering para athletes
Biographical Information

Danielle "Dani" Aravich was born and raised in Boise, Idaho, without her left hand and forearm. Aravich competed in many sports growing up, like soccer, softball, and track, before heading to Indianapolis to attend Butler University as a member of the cross-country and track team. After her freshman year, dealing with persistent knee injuries, she shifted her attention to academics. 

After graduating with a degree in Marketing and Entrepreneurship, Aravich went to work in professional sports, starting with the Utah Jazz, when, in late 2018, she was told about the Paralympics from a  family friend.  Through her training in track and field, she was asked to attend a development camp for US Para Nordic Skiing in late 2019.

Aravich made her Paralympic debut at the Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020 in the women’s T47 400m. Just over six months later, she would represent Team USA at the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022 in biathlon and cross-country skiing. 

Aravich is nicknamed “Biathlon Bandit,” a nickname bestowed upon her by her biathlon coach.  The name encompasses her newfound love of biathlon but also incorporates her Western U.S. roots. Aravich wears cowboy boots to every race and has a Team USA cowboy hat made for the Winter Paralympics. Dani was a part of the bid process and helped in getting the Olympics and Paralympics back to Salt Lake City for 2034.

Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Milan 2026, 6th (12.5km Individual - Standing - Women, 1km Sprint Classic Style - Standing - Women), 7th (20km Freestyle - Standing - Women), 10th (7.5km Sprint - Standing - Women, Sprint Pursuit - Standing - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, 8th (1km Sprint Freestyle - Standing - Women), 9th (10km Freestyle - Standing - Women), 11th (10km Middle - Standing - Women), 13th (6km Individual - Standing - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, 10th (400m T47 - Women)

  • Years of participation: 10km Classic Style - Standing - Women 2022, 2025; 12.5km Individual - Standing - Women 2022, 2023, 2025; 1km Sprint Classic Style - Standing - Women 2022, 2025; 20km Freestyle - Standing - Women 2025; 7.5km Sprint - Standing - Women 2023, 2025; Sprint Pursuit - Standing - Women 2025; 1km Sprint Freestyle - Standing - Women 2023; 4 x 2.5km Relay Mixed - Mixed 2022, 2023; 10km Middle - Standing - Women 2022; 6km Individual - Standing - Women 2022
